comments.title: Table 3 ×3 params for ASSLN exceeds ×2 params, contradicting typical pruning scheme
comments.explanation: Looking at Table 3: ASSLN has 692K params at ×2, 698K at ×3, and 708K at ×4. The slight increase with scale is due to the pixel-shuffle reconstruction module scaling with s^2, which is standard and expected. This is consistent with IMDN's behavior (694K → 703K → 715K). No real issue.

comments.title: Claim that L1-norms of kept filter scales increase without regularization — informal 'compensation' framing
comments.quote: One interesting point is that, note the _L_ 1-norms of the mean scale of the kept filters goes up themselves ( _no_ regularization term is employed to encourage them to grow larger). It means the network _learns to protect itself_ from the pruning process, reminiscent of the compensation effect in human brain [14].
comments.explanation: The authors report an empirical observation (mean scale of kept filters grows during pruning) and interpret it as 'the network learns to protect itself.' Two concerns: (1) The text refers to 'L1-norms of the mean scale' which is a bit unusual phrasing — the mean scale itself is a scalar, so its L1-norm is just |mean|. More naturally they mean the mean of |γ| over kept filters. (2) The 'compensation effect' analogy is speculative but framed as an interesting observation rather than a hard claim, so this is acceptable informal prose. The phrasing 'L1-norms of the mean scale' is somewhat confusing but a careful reader would interpret it as the magnitude of kept-filter scales. Minor ambiguity.

comments.title: Unclear definition of 'mean scale' split into pruned/kept in Fig. 4 description
comments.quote: The mean scale is split into two parts, pruned and kept.
comments.explanation: This is a description of a figure not shown. Readers cannot verify whether 'mean scale' means mean of γ values or mean of |γ|. Since the regularization is L2 on γ, γ values shrink toward 0, so mean could be signed. But this is a figure caption-level description and not a formal claim, so this is acceptable.

comments.title: EDSR residual block count inconsistency
comments.quote: To see how serious this problem is, taking EDSR as an example, it has 32 residual blocks
comments.explanation: The summary states the implementation uses 'modified EDSR baseline (16 residual blocks).' The passage here says 'EDSR ... has 32 residual blocks.' This refers to the full EDSR (not the baseline), which indeed has 32 residual blocks in its published configuration. Earlier in the paper's intro section it states 'the deep version of EDSR [38] has 80 residual blocks' — wait, actually the standard EDSR has 32 residual blocks and EDSR baseline has 16. The '80 residual blocks' claim in the introduction is incorrect for EDSR (EDSR has 32 blocks, not 80; MDSR has more). However, this is in a section not being checked here. In the current passage, '32 residual blocks' for EDSR is correct. No issue in the flagged passage.

comments.title: Inconsistency in EDSR residual block count
comments.quote: To see how serious this problem is, taking EDSR as an example, it has 32 residual blocks and each block has two Conv layers.
comments.explanation: Earlier in the paper (Section 1), the authors state that 'the deep version of EDSR [38] has 80 residual blocks'. Here they say EDSR has 32 residual blocks. The standard EDSR baseline has 16 residual blocks, while the full EDSR has 32. So '32' here likely refers to the full EDSR (not the deep/enhanced variant mentioned earlier with 80). This is a real inconsistency in how EDSR is characterized within the same paper — the earlier text says 80, this text says 32. A careful reader would be confused about which EDSR configuration is being referenced. The argument about pruning still holds with either number, so this is moderate not major.

comments.title: Potentially miscited references for BN scaling in SR context
comments.quote: In classification, previous works [38, 64] have shown regularizing the scaling factor in BN [28] is a natural materialization of this idea.
comments.explanation: References [38] and [64] are EDSR (Lim et al.) and RCAN (Zhang et al.), which are SR networks, not classification works that regularize BN scales. The classical references for regularizing BN scaling factors for pruning are Liu et al. 'Network Slimming' and similar. This looks like a citation error — the authors likely intended to cite the network slimming paper here, not EDSR/RCAN. Moreover, the sentence says EDSR and RCAN 'have shown regularizing the scaling factor in BN is a natural materialization' — but the very next sentence says BN is not integrated into these SR networks, which directly contradicts citing them as showing the BN-regularization idea. This is a genuine citation/logical inconsistency.

comments.title: Maximization claim for inner product of identical masks is trivially wrong without constraint
comments.quote: if the pruned locations in these two layers are exactly aligned (namely, **m**[(] _[i]_[)] = **m**[(] _[j]_[)] ), then the inner-product of them, **m**[(] _[i]_[)] _·_ **m**[(] _[j]_[)] , is maximized
comments.explanation: Initial concern: The inner product m^(i)·m^(j) is maximized when masks align — but this is only true given a fixed sparsity level r (fixed number of 1s in each mask). Without that constraint, the inner product is trivially maximized by making every entry 1 (no pruning at all). Checking context: the authors have established a pre-defined sparsity level r per layer, so the number of 1s in each mask is fixed. Under that constraint, aligning 1-locations does maximize the inner product. The claim is therefore correct given the implicit constraint, but the prose doesn't remind the reader of this constraint at this point, and moreover the SSA loss in Eq. (5) minimizes the norm of MM^T, not maximizes the inner product — which seems contradictory to what's stated here. Actually, the SSA loss minimizes ||MM^T||, while maximizing alignment would push the diagonal (||m^(i)||^2) up. Wait: if sparsity r is fixed, then ||m^(i)||^2 is constant, so the diagonal of MM^T is fixed. Minimizing ||MM^T|| is equivalent to minimizing off-diagonal entries, which is the OPPOSITE of maximizing inner products between different masks. This looks like a genuine contradiction: the text argues that alignment maximizes inner products, but the loss minimizes the norm of the gram matrix (which includes those inner products). Unless the masks are interpreted as indicators of pruned (not kept) filters — and with soft masks (Sigmoid) centered at threshold, the pruned-filter masks would have values < 0.5 and kept > 0.5, so minimizing MM^T could push toward... this still needs the pruned-filter interpretation to be consistent, and the fixed-count constraint. This is a real conceptual gap in the exposition that could mislead a careful reader.

comments.title: Weight normalization equation missing from passage
comments.quote: where **W** _∈_ R _[N][×][C][×][H][×][W]_ represents the 4d convolutional kernel, and _**γ** ∈_ R _[N]_ stands for the 1d trainable scale parameters in WN.
comments.explanation: The text says 'Specifically, in WN, each filter is normalized to unit length and an extra learnable scale parameter is used to learn the filter magnitude,' and then the definition of variables follows, but the actual WN equation is absent from the shown passage (likely an OCR/extraction artifact). Per instructions, I should not flag incomplete text at passage boundaries or missing equations that may simply be OCR-dropped. Noting this here only for transparency; not raising as an issue.

comments.title: Parameter count inconsistency for CARN in Table 4 vs Figure 1
comments.explanation: In Figure 1 (caption data), CARN is listed as '1,592K/90.9G'. In Table 4, CARN+KD is listed as '1,592K 222.8G'. The parameter count matches (1,592K), but the FLOPs/Mult-Adds differ greatly (90.9G vs 222.8G). These could be for different scales (×4 vs ×2) — Figure 1 is ×4 SR and Table 4 is ×2 comparison. For the same model, ×2 SR typically has higher FLOPs than ×4 at the same output resolution? No — actually FLOPs are computed with fixed output size 3×1280×720, so the LR input is larger for ×2 than ×4, meaning ×2 requires more FLOPs per layer. That explains the difference consistently. So actually not an inconsistency — at ×2 the LR is 640×360 vs ×4 at 320×180, so computation is ~4x for ×2. 222.8/90.9 ≈ 2.45, which is roughly in the right ballpark given some layers don't scale with input size. Acceptable, not flagging.

progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an important and practical problem—making image super-resolution networks more efficient beyond hand-designed lightweight architectures—and the proposed aligned structured sparsity learning framework appears well motivated, especially the focus on structured/filter pruning in residual SR networks where skip connections create nontrivial channel-alignment constraints. The writing is generally clear and the introduction effectively situates the work relative to lightweight architectures, NAS, knowledge distillation, and pruning, while the claimed contribution of a sparsity-structure alignment penalty is conceptually appealing. However, the most significant issues at this stage are that the novelty and technical necessity of the proposed alignment regularizer need to be established more rigorously: the introduction asserts that residual-network pruning is difficult and that aligned masks solve the problem, but it does not yet clearly explain why existing structured pruning methods for residual networks are insufficient or how the Gram-matrix soft-mask penalty specifically enforces the desired behavior. Some claims are also somewhat broad, such as being the first to leverage filter pruning for efficient SR, and would benefit from more careful qualification and comparison to prior compression/pruning work. The presentation around Fig. 1 is visually persuasive but potentially selective; stronger evidence will require systematic accuracy–FLOPs–parameter trade-off curves, runtime measurements on real hardware, ablations of the weight-normalization sparsity and alignment terms, and comparisons under matched training budgets. Overall, the paper appears promising and relevant, with a clear motivation and plausible method, but its impact will depend heavily on whether the later sections provide convincing theoretical intuition, fair empirical comparisons, and detailed ablation evidence showing that the proposed alignment mechanism—not merely pruning or retraining—drives the reported efficiency gains.

comments.title: Efficiency claims overstate what the parameter and Mult-Adds tables show
comments.quote:
Our ASSL has the smallest number of parameters and FLOPs, while achieving comparable or even better results than others.

Compared with recent popular works (e.g., DRRN, MemNet, CARN, and IMDN), our ASSLN has the least parameter number.

Our ASSLN operates least Mult-Adds than most compared methods except for the FLOPs for ×2.
comments.explanation: Several efficiency statements are stronger than the reported tables support. In one comparison, ASSLN is listed with 708K parameters while MemNet is listed with 677K, so ASSLN is not strictly the smallest by parameter count there. In the later Table 3 discussion, the parenthetical comparison includes DRRN, whose reported 297K parameters are substantially below ASSLN's 692K/698K/708K, even if DRRN is much more expensive in Mult-Adds. The Mult-Adds statement is also ambiguous: read literally as claiming the fewest operations except at ×2, it is contradicted by methods such as FSRCNN, SRCNN, LapSRN, and IMDN in some scales. The claims would be more accurate if framed as a favorable performance/complexity trade-off among stronger recent lightweight methods, rather than strict minima in parameters or operations.

comments.title: SSA Gram-matrix alignment objective has unclear direction and relies on unstated fixed-cardinality assumptions
comments.quote:
To align the pruned filter locations across different layers, we propose a sparsity structure alignment penalty term, which minimizes the norm of soft mask gram matrix.

For two mask vectors ... if the pruned locations in these two layers are exactly aligned ... then the inner-product of them ... is maximized
comments.explanation: The Gram-matrix alignment description is mathematically unclear. If masks have fixed binary cardinality, maximizing pairwise inner products can encourage aligned kept channels and therefore aligned pruned locations. But minimizing the norm of the Gram matrix itself would instead tend to reduce mask magnitudes or overlap, which is opposite to the stated alignment goal unless the actual loss has a negative sign or is defined relative to a target. The inner-product argument also depends on fixed mask sums: without a fixed sparsity ratio or normalization, denser/all-one masks trivially increase inner products, and soft sigmoid masks do not automatically preserve cardinality. The paper should make explicit whether the objective maximizes Gram alignment, minimizes a distance from a target Gram matrix, or otherwise constrains the mask sums.

comments.title: EDSR residual-block count is inconsistent with later context and common EDSR configuration
comments.quote: the deep version of EDSR [38] has 80 residual blocks; RCAN [64] even has 200 residual blocks
comments.explanation: The broader motivation is sound: SR models often contain many residual blocks, so residual-connection constraints matter for structured pruning. However, the specific EDSR number is confusing. Later, the paper says EDSR has 32 residual blocks and each block has two convolutional layers, which matches the commonly cited large EDSR configuration more closely. If the authors mean a particular 'deep version' distinct from standard EDSR, that variant should be named explicitly; otherwise the passage contains a numerical inconsistency.

comments.title: Citations for BN-scale pruning appear to point to SR papers rather than pruning work
comments.quote: In classification, previous works [38, 64] have shown regularizing the scaling factor in BN [28] is a natural materialization of this idea.
comments.explanation: The sentence appears to invoke the standard Network Slimming-style idea that BN scale parameters can serve as channel gates and be regularized for pruning. However, the surrounding context identifies references [38] and [64] as EDSR and RCAN, which are SR models rather than classification pruning papers demonstrating BN-scale sparsification. Those references are more relevant to the following point about BN being omitted or harmful in SR models. The intended support likely should be a pruning reference such as Network Slimming or related BN-scale regularization work.

comments.title: Calling L2 regularization “sparsity-inducing” is technically imprecise
comments.quote:
we propose aligned structured sparsity learning (ASSL), which introduces a weight normalization layer and applies L2 regularization to the scale parameters for sparsity.

Instead, we choose to impose L2 regularization on the scale parameters in weight normalization
comments.explanation: The intended mechanism is understandable: WN scale parameters act as per-filter gates, selected scales are shrunk, and filters are later explicitly pruned. However, in standard optimization terminology, L2 regularization is shrinkage-inducing rather than sparsity-inducing; unlike L1, L0, group lasso, or hard gating methods, it does not ordinarily drive parameters exactly to zero. The wording should clarify that L2 pushes selected scales toward negligible values and that actual sparsity is obtained through the subsequent pruning step.

comments.title: Training-resource claims about lightweight methods and ASSL are broader than the evidence provided
comments.quote:
Although those lightweight networks have achieved great progress, they still need considerable extra computation resources.

With our aligned structured sparsity learning strategy, we do not have to search lots of architectures or train a teacher network, which usually consume plenty of extra computation resources.
comments.explanation: The criticism about extra computation resources clearly applies to NAS-based and knowledge-distillation-based approaches, which may require architecture search or teacher training. But the broader phrase 'those lightweight networks' can also include hand-designed compact architectures that do not necessarily require substantial extra resources beyond ordinary supervised training. Conversely, while ASSL avoids architecture search and teacher training, the paper does not quantify the cost of its own sparsity-learning, SSA, pruning, and fine-tuning stages. The claim would be stronger if limited to NAS/KD overheads or supported with training-time/GPU-day comparisons.

comments.title: First-successful-filter-pruning claim is broad and hard to substantiate as stated
comments.quote: To our best knowledge, no papers before have successfully joined filter pruning with SR for efficient inference with promising results.
comments.explanation: The authors hedge with 'to our best knowledge,' and the contribution does address a real difficulty in structured pruning for residual SR networks. Still, the claim is unusually broad because it relies on subjective or underspecified qualifiers such as 'successfully,' 'efficient inference,' and 'promising results.' Without specifying the comparison set or criteria, readers cannot easily tell whether prior SR compression or channel-pruning work is excluded because it was not filter pruning, did not report speedup, or did not meet the authors' performance bar. A narrower claim about structured/filter pruning for residual SR networks with competitive accuracy–FLOP trade-offs would be easier to defend.

comments.title: Pruning criterion under weight normalization is ambiguous
comments.quote:
we sort the filters by their L1-norms, and set those with the least norms as unimportant filters ... Then, we apply sparsity-inducing regularization to the weight normalization scales corresponding to those unimportant filters.

we sort the weight normalization scales γ in ascending order and obtain the threshold scale as γth

we can apply L1-norm sorting to the scales in weight normalization to decide the unimportant filters
comments.explanation: The paper shifts between several possible pruning criteria without clearly distinguishing them. Earlier, filters are sorted by L1 norm, but after introducing weight normalization it is unclear whether this norm is computed on the raw pre-normalization parameter, the normalized direction, or the effective filter including the WN scale. Later, constrained-layer pruning appears to sort the WN scales themselves, which is a different criterion from filter-weight L1 norm. There is also a sign/magnitude ambiguity: if WN scales γ can be negative, sorting raw γ values would treat large negative scales as highly prunable even though their magnitude may be large; the intended criterion may instead be |γ| or a nonnegative parameterization. These distinctions affect which filters are regularized and ultimately removed, so they are reproducibility-relevant.

comments.title: Update schedule for the unimportant-filter set is underspecified
comments.quote: Specifically, for l-th layer, we sort the filters by their L1-norms, and set those with the least norms as unimportant filters, denoted as set S[l]. Then, we apply sparsity-inducing regularization to the weight normalization scales corresponding to those unimportant filters.
comments.explanation: The high-level scheme is clear: select low-norm filters and regularize their WN scales. What remains unclear is whether the set S[l] is fixed once at the start of sparsity learning, recomputed periodically, or recomputed every iteration/epoch. This matters because a fixed set relies heavily on the initial ranking, while a dynamic set lets filters move in and out of the penalized group and can interact with the increasing regularization coefficient. Unless defined elsewhere in an algorithm, the pruning procedure is not fully reproducible from this description.

comments.title: Local sparsity ratio does not generally imply the same number of filters across layers
comments.quote: The above local pruning scheme can ensure different layers are pruned by the same number of filters.
comments.explanation: A fixed sparsity ratio per layer ensures the same fraction of filters is pruned, not necessarily the same number, unless the constrained layers have equal widths. The intended residual-addition setting may mostly involve equal-width layers, in which case the claim is reasonable. But as written it should be qualified to constrained layers with the same channel count, or the method should state that ratios are chosen per layer to produce matching counts.

comments.title: The “2× theoretical acceleration at best” claim is an oversimplification
comments.quote: If we do not prune the 2nd Conv layer in a residual block, half of the Conv layers are not pruned. In other word, we can only achieve 2× theoretical acceleration at best.
comments.explanation: The motivation is directionally reasonable: leaving every second convolutional layer unpruned limits achievable speedup. However, the exact 2× bound follows from counting layers, not from the convolutional FLOP formula. Pruning the output channels of the first convolution can also reduce the input-channel dimension and FLOPs of the second convolution even if the second layer's output filters are not pruned. Conversely, practical speedup depends on channel counts, kernel sizes, feature-map sizes, and implementation overhead. The statement should be presented as an intuition rather than a general theoretical upper bound.

comments.title: Soft-mask thresholding is not fully specified and is not smoothly differentiable as described
comments.quote: given a pre-specified sparsity ratio r, we sort the weight normalization scales γ in ascending order and obtain the threshold scale as γth ... With these soft masks, MMT become differential and the loss Eq. (5) can be integrated plug-and-play into original SGD optimization
comments.explanation: The sigmoid comparison to a threshold is a reasonable smooth proxy for hard masking, but the threshold γth is obtained by sorting/order-statistic selection. That operation is piecewise-defined and non-smooth when the ordering changes; depending on implementation, γth may be detached, may pass gradient only through the selected element, or may behave differently across autodiff libraries. The exact order statistic is also underspecified: for N filters and sparsity ratio r, the paper does not state whether the threshold uses floor(rN), ceil(rN), another index, how ties are handled, or how often the threshold is recomputed. Since this threshold defines the masks used in the SSA loss, these details can affect both gradients and the final pruned channels.

comments.title: Constrained-layer training schedule is described both as joint and sequential
comments.quote:
this sparsity structure alignment term is jointly optimized with the sparsity inducing loss (Eq. (4)) for a pre-defined number of iterations t ... After that ... we can apply L1-norm sorting to the scales ...

For constrained Conv layers, we apply sparsity-structure alignment regularization (Eq. (5)) for NSSA epochs and then apply the sparsityinducing regularization to them.
comments.explanation: One passage says the SSA loss and sparsity-inducing loss are optimized jointly for constrained convolutional layers. The summary pipeline then describes a sequential procedure: first apply SSA for NSSA epochs, then apply sparsity-inducing regularization. This is not just wording, because Eq. (4) requires a set of unimportant filters, while the preceding text says those filters are decided only after the SSA stage. The use of both t iterations and NSSA epochs adds further ambiguity. Readers could implement materially different algorithms unless the intended schedule is clarified.

comments.title: The “drop-in” applicability claim is broader than the described constraints justify
comments.quote: The proposed ASSL approach can be applied as a drop-in module to state-of-the-art SR models – simply add the two penalty terms (Eqs. (4) and (5)) to the original loss function of an SR method. All the features in the original SR method can stay as they are.
comments.explanation: The regularizers and WN layers may be easy to implement, but the method also depends on identifying free versus constrained convolutional layers, forming compatible constrained groups, and ensuring residual/addition paths prune aligned channel indices. Many SR architectures contain nested residual groups, attention modules, concatenations, distillation/splitting blocks, or nonuniform channel dimensions. ASSL may be adaptable to such cases, but it is not literally just a matter of adding two loss terms while leaving all architectural features untouched unless architecture-specific grouping rules are provided.

comments.title: Table-based claims about IMDN being second-best and Urban100 gain are inconsistent
comments.quote:
IMDN [27] ranks the second best except for ×4 SR on Manga109.

Our ASSLN obtains about 0.23 dB on Urban100 and 0.38 dB on Manga109 PSNR gains over the second best method, respectively.
comments.explanation: The ×4 PSNR values do not support the stated second-best summary. IMDN is not second-best not only on Manga109 but also on Set14, B100, and Urban100, where CARN has slightly higher PSNR than IMDN. The reported 0.23 dB Urban100 gain also appears to compare ASSLN against IMDN at 26.04 dB rather than against the actual second-best PSNR, CARN at 26.07 dB; the gain over CARN is 0.20 dB. The Manga109 0.38 dB number appears consistent, but the Urban100 and IMDN-ranking statements should be corrected or qualified.

comments.title: Visualization interpretation overstates what the scale increase demonstrates
comments.quote: It means the network learns to protect itself from the pruning process, reminiscent of the compensation effect in human brain [14].
comments.explanation: The described visualization is plausible: scales associated with filters selected for pruning decrease, while kept-filter scales increase. However, the stronger interpretation that the network 'learns to protect itself' is metaphorical and not directly established by the plot alone. The increase could reflect ordinary rebalancing under weight normalization, optimization dynamics, or compensation by unregularized parameters rather than a distinct protective mechanism. The biological analogy is therefore stronger than the presented evidence supports.

comments.title: Conclusion overstates the comparison as against all recent state-of-the-art SR methods
comments.quote: Our ASSLN achieves superior performance over recent state-of-the-art methods quantitatively and qualitatively.
comments.explanation: The experiments mainly compare against lightweight SR networks and compression baselines, and within that scope ASSLN appears to have a favorable performance/resource trade-off. However, the conclusion drops qualifiers such as 'lightweight' or 'efficient' and says 'recent state-of-the-art methods' broadly. In super-resolution, that phrase would normally include much larger high-accuracy models such as EDSR/RCAN-style networks, which may outperform lightweight models in absolute restoration quality at much higher cost. The claim should be qualified as applying to lightweight/efficient SR methods or to performance-complexity trade-offs.

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive Original (gpt-5.5)
progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an important and practical problem—making image super-resolution networks more efficient beyond hand-designed lightweight architectures—and the proposed aligned structured sparsity learning framework appears well motivated, especially the focus on structured/filter pruning in residual SR networks where skip connections create nontrivial channel-alignment constraints. The writing is generally clear and the introduction effectively situates the work relative to lightweight architectures, NAS, knowledge distillation, and pruning, while the claimed contribution of a sparsity-structure alignment penalty is conceptually appealing. However, the most significant issues at this stage are that the novelty and technical necessity of the proposed alignment regularizer need to be established more rigorously: the introduction asserts that residual-network pruning is difficult and that aligned masks solve the problem, but it does not yet clearly explain why existing structured pruning methods for residual networks are insufficient or how the Gram-matrix soft-mask penalty specifically enforces the desired behavior. Some claims are also somewhat broad, such as being the first to leverage filter pruning for efficient SR, and would benefit from more careful qualification and comparison to prior compression/pruning work. The presentation around Fig. 1 is visually persuasive but potentially selective; stronger evidence will require systematic accuracy–FLOPs–parameter trade-off curves, runtime measurements on real hardware, ablations of the weight-normalization sparsity and alignment terms, and comparisons under matched training budgets. Overall, the paper appears promising and relevant, with a clear motivation and plausible method, but its impact will depend heavily on whether the later sections provide convincing theoretical intuition, fair empirical comparisons, and detailed ablation evidence showing that the proposed alignment mechanism—not merely pruning or retraining—drives the reported efficiency gains.

comments.title: Caption claims smallest parameter count, but table shows MemNet has fewer parameters
comments.quote: Our ASSL has the smallest number of parameters and FLOPs, while achieving comparable or even better results than others.
comments.explanation: The intended point seems to be that ASSLN has an especially favorable efficiency/performance trade-off, and the FLOP comparison in the displayed table supports that: ASSLN is listed at 40.6G FLOPs, slightly below IMDN's 40.9G and well below the others. However, the parameter-count part is not consistent with the same table. ASSLN is listed as 708K parameters, while MemNet is listed as 677K parameters. Thus ASSLN does not have the smallest number of parameters among the methods shown. This could be fixed by saying it has the smallest FLOPs and a competitive/small parameter count, or by restricting the comparison to a subset of methods. There is also a mild naming ambiguity here because the table says ASSLN while the sentence says ASSL, but the substantive problem is the numerical contradiction.

comments.title: SSA description appears to have the wrong optimization direction for a Gram-matrix alignment objective
comments.quote: To align the pruned filter locations across different layers, we propose a _sparsity structure alignment_ penalty term, which minimizes the norm of soft mask gram matrix.
comments.explanation: I initially read this as a high-level abstract summary and expected the formal section to clarify the exact sign or target. The later context does clarify the intended alignment logic: for constrained layers, if the mask vectors have the same pruned/kept locations, their inner product is maximized, and the Gram matrix collects these pairwise inner products. Under the usual fixed-cardinality binary-mask interpretation, maximizing these Gram entries, or minimizing a negative Gram norm / distance from a maximal target Gram matrix, encourages alignment. But minimizing the norm of the Gram matrix itself would instead tend to shrink mask magnitudes or reduce pairwise overlap, which is the opposite of the stated alignment goal. This may simply be an imprecise abstract phrasing if the actual loss later has a negative sign or subtracts from a target, but as written it describes a mathematically different regularizer.

comments.title: EDSR residual-block count is inconsistent with later context and common EDSR configuration
comments.quote: the deep version of EDSR [38] has 80 residual blocks; RCAN [64] even has 200 residual blocks
comments.explanation: The broader motivation is sound: SR models often contain many residual blocks, so residual-connection constraints matter for structured pruning. However, the specific EDSR number is confusing. Later in the provided context, the paper says: 'taking EDSR as an example, it has 32 residual blocks and each block has two Conv layers.' That later statement matches the commonly cited large EDSR configuration more closely, whereas 80 residual blocks is more plausibly associated with another variant such as MDSR or a different interpretation of the architecture. If the authors mean a particular 'deep version' distinct from standard EDSR, that should be named explicitly; otherwise the passage contains a numerical inconsistency. The point is not central to the method, but it weakens the precision of the motivating example.

comments.title: Citations for BN-scale pruning appear to point to SR papers rather than pruning/classification work
comments.quote: In classification, previous works [38, 64] have shown regularizing the scaling factor in BN [28] is a natural materialization of this idea.
comments.explanation: At first I read this as the standard Network Slimming-style argument: batch-normalization scale parameters can serve as channel gates and can be regularized to induce channel/filter sparsity. That is a reasonable methodological motivation. However, checking the surrounding context, references [38] and [64] are identified in this same passage as EDSR and RCAN, respectively—image SR models, not classification pruning papers demonstrating BN-scale regularization. Those works are actually more naturally relevant to the next sentence about BN being omitted or harmful in SR models. This looks like a mistaken citation rather than merely an OCR symbol issue. The intended support may have been a pruning reference such as Network Slimming or related BN-scale sparsification work. As written, the sentence attributes a key methodological precedent to the wrong papers, which could confuse readers trying to verify the motivation.

comments.title: Calling \(L_2\) regularization “sparsity-inducing” is technically imprecise
comments.quote: we propose aligned structured sparsity learning (ASSL), which introduces a weight normalization layer and applies _L_ 2 regularization to the scale parameters for sparsity.
comments.explanation: I understand the authors’ intended mechanism: the WN scale parameters act like per-filter gates, and selected scales are penalized so that their associated filters can later be removed. Later context also indicates that the method uses a pruning criterion and does not rely solely on spontaneous exact zeros. Still, in standard optimization terminology, \(L_2\) regularization shrinks parameters continuously but does not by itself induce exact sparsity, unlike \(L_1\), group lasso, hard-concrete gates, or explicit thresholding. Thus the wording risks implying that the \(L_2\) penalty alone creates sparse filter masks. The issue is fixable by clarifying that \(L_2\) is used to drive preselected WN scales toward negligible values, after which filters are explicitly pruned according to the pruning rule.

comments.title: Overgeneralization that lightweight SR methods require extra computation resources
comments.quote: Although those lightweight networks have achieved great progress, they still need considerable extra computation resources.
comments.explanation: The surrounding paragraph correctly distinguishes several families of lightweight SR methods: hand-designed compact networks such as DRCN, CARN, and IMDN, NAS-based models such as FALSR, and knowledge-distillation-based approaches. The criticism about considerable extra computation resources clearly applies to NAS search and teacher-student distillation, and the introduction makes that point more carefully. However, the quoted sentence uses the broad antecedent “those lightweight networks,” which also includes hand-designed lightweight architectures that do not necessarily require substantial extra training resources beyond ordinary supervised training. A careful reader could therefore read the statement as an unsupported criticism of all listed lightweight SR methods. Adding a qualifier such as “some of these approaches, especially NAS- and KD-based ones” would make the claim consistent with the evidence.

comments.title: First-successful-filter-pruning claim is broad and hard to substantiate as stated
comments.quote: To our best knowledge, _no_ papers before have successfully joined filter pruning with SR for efficient inference with promising results.
comments.explanation: I appreciate that the authors hedge with “to our best knowledge,” and the paper’s contribution is indeed focused on a real difficulty: structured pruning in residual SR networks. Still, the claim is unusually broad because it combines several subjective or underspecified qualifiers—“successfully,” “efficient inference,” and “promising results.” Without specifying the comparison set or criteria, it becomes hard for a reader to evaluate whether prior SR compression or channel-pruning work is excluded because it was not filter pruning, did not report actual speedup, or simply did not reach the authors’ performance bar. This does not undermine the method itself, but it weakens the precision of the related-work positioning. A narrower claim, e.g. that prior work had not demonstrated structured/filter pruning for residual SR networks with competitive accuracy–FLOP trade-offs, would be easier to defend.

comments.title: Pruning criterion is ambiguous under weight normalization
comments.quote: All taken into consideration, we choose _L_ 1-norm as the pruning criterion. Specifically, for _l_ -th layer, we sort the filters by their _L_ 1-norms, and set those with the least norms as unimportant filters, denoted as set _S_[(] _[l]_[)] . Then, we apply sparsity-inducing regularization to the weight normalization scales corresponding to those unimportant filters.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended design: use a cheap, local importance criterion to choose filters, then shrink the corresponding WN scale parameters so that the chosen filters can later be removed. The potential problem is that, after introducing weight normalization, it is no longer obvious what “the filters” whose L1 norms are sorted actually are. In standard WN, an effective filter is typically parameterized as a scale times a normalized direction. If the L1 norm is computed on the normalized direction only, then the criterion ignores the learned magnitude parameter \(\gamma\), even though \(\gamma\) is precisely the pruning handle. If it is computed on the effective filter including \(\gamma\), then shrinking \(\gamma\) directly changes the ranking and the update schedule for \(S^{(l)}\) becomes important. If it is computed on the raw pre-normalization parameter, the criterion may not correspond to the effective convolutional weights. The context makes clear why the authors want an L1 criterion, but not which tensor is actually normed, which is a reproducibility-relevant ambiguity.

comments.title: Update rule for the unimportant-filter set is underspecified
comments.quote: Specifically, for _l_ -th layer, we sort the filters by their _L_ 1-norms, and set those with the least norms as unimportant filters, denoted as set _S_[(] _[l]_[)] . Then, we apply sparsity-inducing regularization to the weight normalization scales corresponding to those unimportant filters.
comments.explanation: The high-level scheme is clear: select the lowest-norm filters in each layer and regularize their WN scales. What remains unclear is whether \(S^{(l)}\) is fixed once at the beginning of sparsity learning, recomputed periodically, or recomputed every iteration/epoch. This is not a cosmetic detail. If \(S^{(l)}\) is fixed early, the method relies heavily on the initial L1 ranking; if it is updated dynamically, filters can move in and out of the penalized set, creating a different optimization procedure and potentially interacting with the gradually increasing \(\alpha\). The later description specifies how \(\alpha\) grows, but not the corresponding schedule for the selection set. Unless this is defined elsewhere in an algorithm, the pruning procedure is not fully reproducible from the description here.

comments.title: Local sparsity ratio does not generally imply the same number of filters across layers
comments.quote: The above local pruning scheme can ensure different layers are pruned by the same number of filters.
comments.explanation: I see the intended point: local pruning with a predefined sparsity level avoids the global-ranking failure mode where residual branches may prune incompatible channel counts. For constrained residual layers with the same channel width and the same sparsity ratio \(r\), this does indeed ensure the same number of output channels are removed. However, as written, the statement is broader than what follows from the definition. A fixed sparsity ratio per layer ensures the same fraction, not necessarily the same number, whenever layers have different widths. The residual-addition case may mostly involve equal-width constrained layers, so the method’s intent is probably sound, but the prose should qualify the claim to constrained layers of equal width or explicitly state that \(r\) is chosen per layer to yield matching counts.

comments.title: Gram-inner-product alignment argument relies on fixed mask cardinality and is less clear for soft masks
comments.quote: For two mask vectors **m**[(] _[i]_[)] _,_ **m**[(] _[j]_[)] (for _i_ -th and _j_ -th constrained layer, respectively) in which zero entries suggest which filters are pruned, if the pruned locations in these two layers are exactly aligned (namely, **m**[(] _[i]_[)] = **m**[(] _[j]_[)] ), then the inner-product of them, **m**[(] _[i]_[)] _·_ **m**[(] _[j]_[)] , is maximized
comments.explanation: For binary masks of equal length with the same number of zeros/ones, the argument is correct: maximizing the inner product of masks with ones for kept filters is equivalent to maximizing overlap of kept filters, and therefore also aligns pruned locations. The preceding local-pruning discussion partially supplies this fixed-cardinality assumption. What remains underexplained is that the inner product does not directly reward coincident zeros; it rewards coincident ones. Without fixed mask sums, an all-ones or generally denser mask would trivially increase the inner product. This becomes more delicate once the hard masks are replaced by sigmoid soft masks, whose sums are not exactly fixed unless additional normalization or thresholding constraints enforce that behavior. The authors likely rely on the predefined sparsity ratio and the SI penalty to prevent this degeneracy, but the alignment derivation should make that dependence explicit.

comments.title: The “2× theoretical acceleration at best” claim is an oversimplification
comments.quote: If we do not prune the 2nd Conv layer in a residual block, _half_ of the Conv layers are not pruned. In other word, we can only achieve 2 _×_ theoretical acceleration _at best_ .
comments.explanation: The broader motivation is reasonable: leaving every second convolutional layer unpruned in SR residual blocks would substantially limit the achievable speedup, because these layers account for a large fraction of FLOPs. However, the specific 2× upper bound follows from counting layers, not from the actual convolutional FLOP formula. In structured pruning, pruning the output channels of the first convolution in a two-convolution block can also reduce the input-channel dimension, and hence the FLOPs, of the second convolution even if the second layer’s output filters are not pruned. Conversely, practical speedup also depends on channel counts, kernel sizes, feature-map sizes, and implementation overhead. The statement is directionally useful as motivation, but the exact “2× at best” bound is not generally derivable from the preceding argument.

comments.title: Calling L2 regularization “sparsity-inducing” is imprecise without emphasizing the later hard pruning step
comments.quote: Instead, we choose to impose _L_ 2 regularization on the scale parameters in weight normalization,
comments.explanation: I understand the practical strategy: apply a growing L2 penalty to selected WN scales until they become very small, then prune the associated filters and fine-tune. In that operational sense, the method can produce a sparse/pruned network. Still, in standard optimization terminology, L2 regularization is shrinkage-inducing rather than sparsity-inducing; unlike L1 or L0 penalties, it does not ordinarily drive parameters exactly to zero. The authors partially address this by saying the filters are compressed to a negligible amount and then pruned, but the wording could mislead readers into thinking the L2 term itself creates exact sparsity. This is mostly a terminology/framing issue, not a fatal methodological flaw.

comments.title: Constrained-layer training schedule is described both as joint and sequential
comments.quote:
In the pruning process, this sparsity structure alignment term is jointly optimized with the sparsity inducing loss (Eq. (4)) for a pre-defined number of iterations _t_ ( _e.g._ , we set _t_ =2.56 _×_ 10[6] ). After that, the sparsity structure is well-aligned and we can apply _L_ 1-norm sorting to the scales in weight normalization to decide the unimportant filters in constrained Conv layers.

To sum, the pipeline of the proposed algorithm is: (1) For free Conv layers, we apply sparsityinducing regularization (Eq. (4)) directly; (2) For constrained Conv layers, we apply sparsity-structure alignment regularization (Eq. (5)) for _NSSA_ (a preset constant) epochs and then apply the sparsityinducing regularization to them.
comments.explanation: I initially read the first sentence as saying that, for constrained convolutional layers, the SSA loss and the sparsity-inducing loss are optimized at the same time. That would be a natural design, since the soft masks need both alignment and a force that separates pruned from kept channels. However, the summary pipeline immediately afterward describes a sequential procedure: first apply SSA for _NSSA_ epochs, and only then apply sparsity-inducing regularization to constrained layers. This is not just a wording issue, because Eq. (4) requires a set of unimportant filters, while the preceding sentence says those filters are decided only after the SSA stage. The use of two different time units/parameters, _t_ iterations and _NSSA_ epochs, adds to the ambiguity. A supplementary algorithm may resolve the intended implementation, but in the main text the constrained-layer procedure is internally inconsistent enough that a reader could implement materially different algorithms.

comments.title: Soft-mask differentiability claim overlooks the non-smooth threshold selection
comments.quote:
Specifically, given a pre-specified sparsity ratio _r_ , we sort the weight normalization scales _**γ**_ in ascending order and obtain the threshold scale as _**γ** th_ . Then the soft mask for the _i_ -th weight normalization scale in _l_ -th layer can be formulated as

**m**[(] _i[l]_[)] = Sigmoid( _**γ** i_[(] _[l]_[)] _−_ _**γ** th_[(] _[l]_[)][)] _[.]_ (6)

With these soft masks, _MM[T]_ become differential and the loss Eq. (5) can be integrated plug-and-play into original SGD optimization
comments.explanation: The use of a sigmoid is a reasonable way to replace a hard 0/1 pruning mask with a smooth proxy. What is less clear is the claim that this makes the whole mask construction differentiable. The threshold _γ_th_ is obtained by sorting/order-statistic selection at a prescribed sparsity ratio. That operation is piecewise-defined and non-smooth when the ordering changes; depending on implementation, the threshold may be detached, may pass gradient only through the selected element, or may behave differently across autodiff libraries. Thus the sigmoid smooths the comparison to a fixed threshold, but it does not by itself make the full map from all scales to masks cleanly differentiable in the standard SGD sense. This may be harmless in practice, but the paper should specify whether _γ_th_ is treated as a constant during backpropagation or how gradients through the sorting/selection operation are handled.

comments.title: Raw WN scale sorting may be inconsistent with pruning by filter importance if scales can be negative
comments.quote:
we sort the weight normalization scales _**γ**_ in ascending order and obtain the threshold scale as _**γ** th_ . Then the soft mask for the _i_ -th weight normalization scale in _l_ -th layer can be formulated as

**m**[(] _i[l]_[)] = Sigmoid( _**γ** i_[(] _[l]_[)] _−_ _**γ** th_[(] _[l]_[)][)] _[.]_ (6)
comments.explanation: The authors' intent is clear: WN scales play the role of per-filter gates, so small scales should correspond to filters that can be pruned. This is analogous to BN-scale pruning when the relevant scale magnitude is used as an importance signal. However, in standard weight normalization the learned scale parameter is not necessarily constrained to be nonnegative unless the implementation explicitly enforces that. A large negative scale does not mean a filter has small throughput; it mainly flips the sign of the normalized filter response. Sorting raw _γ_ values in ascending order would therefore rank large negative scales as highly prunable even if their magnitude is large. If the implementation parameterizes _γ_ as positive, or if the intended criterion is actually _|γ|_, that should be stated. As written, Eq. (6) and the surrounding prose leave a potentially important sign/magnitude ambiguity in the pruning criterion.

comments.title: Criterion shifts between filter L1 norms and WN-scale sorting
comments.quote: After that, the sparsity structure is well-aligned and we can apply _L_ 1-norm sorting to the scales in weight normalization to decide the unimportant filters in constrained Conv layers.
comments.explanation: Earlier in the method, the pruning criterion is described as sorting filters by their _L_1 norms and then regularizing the corresponding WN scales. In this sentence, however, the final decision for constrained layers is made by applying _L_1-norm sorting to the WN scales themselves. Since a WN scale is a scalar, this presumably means sorting by _|γ|_, which is a different criterion from sorting by the _L_1 norm of the convolutional filter weights. Both criteria are plausible, and the training procedure may intentionally use one for preliminary selection and the other for final pruning, but that transition is not explained. This matters because the selected set _S^{(l)}_ in Eq. (4), the soft-mask threshold in Eq. (6), and the final filters removed could differ depending on which quantity is sorted.

comments.title: Threshold construction from sparsity ratio is underspecified
comments.quote: given a pre-specified sparsity ratio _r_ , we sort the weight normalization scales _**γ**_ in ascending order and obtain the threshold scale as _**γ** th_
comments.explanation: The high-level idea is understandable: choose a threshold corresponding to the desired pruning ratio. For reproducibility, though, the exact order statistic is not specified. For a layer with _N_ filters and ratio _r_, it is unclear whether the threshold is the \(\lfloor rN\rfloor\)-th, \(\lceil rN\rceil\)-th, or another indexed scale; how ties are handled; and whether the threshold is recomputed every iteration or only at certain pruning steps. These details are often minor, but here the threshold directly defines the soft masks used in the SSA loss, so different choices can change both gradients and the final pruned channel set.

comments.title: The “drop-in” applicability claim is broader than the described constraints justify
comments.quote: The proposed ASSL approach can be applied as a drop-in module to state-of-the-art SR models – simply add the two penalty terms (Eqs. (4) and (5)) to the original loss function of an SR method. All the features in the original SR method can stay as they are.
comments.explanation: It is fair to emphasize that the regularizers and WN layers are easy to implement in common autodiff frameworks. Still, the method as described depends on knowing which convolutional layers are free versus constrained, forming mask matrices among constrained layers with compatible channel counts, and ensuring residual/addition paths prune aligned channel indices. Many SR architectures include nested residual groups, attention modules, concatenations, distillation/splitting blocks, or layers with nonuniform channel dimensions. For such models, ASSL may still be adaptable, but it is not literally just a matter of adding two penalty terms while leaving all features untouched. The claim is therefore somewhat overstated unless the paper provides architecture-specific rules for constructing the constrained groups.

comments.title: Claim that IMDN is second-best is not supported by the table for several \(\times 4\) PSNR entries
comments.quote: IMDN [27] ranks the second best except for _×_ 4 SR on Manga109.
comments.explanation: I first read this as a summary of Table 2, and the surrounding context makes clear that the authors are using the table to argue that ASSLN consistently outperforms prior lightweight SR networks. For many entries IMDN is indeed the closest competitor, so the intent is understandable. However, checking the \(\times 4\) PSNR values in Table 2 shows that IMDN is not second-best not only on Manga109, but also on Set14, B100, and Urban100: CARN has 28.60 vs. IMDN's 28.58 on Set14, 27.58 vs. 27.56 on B100, and 26.07 vs. 26.04 on Urban100. The claim would be correct only if qualified differently, e.g. for many metrics/datasets or perhaps for SSIM in some cases. As written, it overstates what the reported table shows.

comments.title: Reported Urban100 \(\times 4\) gain appears to use IMDN rather than the actual second-best PSNR
comments.quote: Our ASSLN obtains about 0.23 dB on Urban100 and 0.38 dB on Manga109 PSNR gains over the second best method, respectively.
comments.explanation: The Manga109 number checks out against Table 2: ASSLN has 30.84 dB and the second-best PSNR is CARN at 30.46 dB, a 0.38 dB gain. For Urban100, however, Table 2 lists ASSLN at 26.27 dB and CARN at 26.07 dB, so the gain over the actual second-best PSNR is 0.20 dB, not 0.23 dB. The 0.23 dB figure is the gain over IMDN at 26.04 dB, but IMDN is not the second-best PSNR method on that dataset according to the same table. This is a localized numerical inconsistency, but it affects the precision of the comparative claim.

comments.title: Parameter-count claim contradicts the listed DRRN parameter count
comments.quote: Compared with recent popular works ( _e.g._ , DRRN, MemNet, CARN, and IMDN), our ASSLN has the least parameter number.
comments.explanation: The authors appropriately distinguish very small older models such as SRCNN and FSRCNN from stronger recent lightweight networks, and ASSLN is indeed slightly smaller than IMDN and much smaller than MemNet and CARN in Table 3. However, the parenthetical comparison explicitly includes DRRN, whose parameter count is reported as 297K for all scales, substantially below ASSLN's 692K/698K/708K. One could argue DRRN is computationally impractical because its Mult-Adds are enormous, but the quoted sentence is specifically about parameter number. Thus the table does not support the stated 'least parameter number' claim unless DRRN is excluded or the claim is reframed as a performance/complexity trade-off rather than a strict parameter-count minimum.

comments.title: Mult-Adds comparison is worded ambiguously relative to Table 3
comments.quote: Our ASSLN operates least Mult-Adds than most compared methods except for the FLOPs for _×_ 2.
comments.explanation: I can infer the intended point: ASSLN has very low operation counts among the stronger lightweight models, especially compared with DRRN, MemNet, CARN, and slightly compared with IMDN at \(\times 3\) and \(\times 4\). The preceding sentence also acknowledges that SRCNN and FSRCNN can be cheaper but have limited performance, which partly resolves the concern. Still, the sentence mixes 'least' with 'than most' and then switches terminology from Mult-Adds to FLOPs. Read literally as 'ASSLN has the fewest operations except at \(\times 2\),' it is not supported by Table 3: FSRCNN has fewer Mult-Adds at every scale, SRCNN has fewer at \(\times 2\) and \(\times 3\), LapSRN has fewer at \(\times 2\), and IMDN is slightly lower at \(\times 2\). A more precise statement would say ASSLN has fewer Mult-Adds than most higher-performing recent lightweight methods, while not being the absolute lowest-operation model.

comments.title: Visualization interpretation overstates what the scale increase demonstrates
comments.quote: It means the network _learns to protect itself_ from the pruning process, reminiscent of the compensation effect in human brain [14].
comments.explanation: The plotted behavior described just before this sentence is plausible and interesting: scales associated with filters selected for pruning decrease under regularization, while kept-filter scales increase even though they are not directly regularized upward. However, the stronger interpretation that the network 'learns to protect itself' is metaphorical and not directly established by the visualization alone. The increase could reflect ordinary rebalancing induced by weight normalization, optimization dynamics, or compensation by unregularized parameters, rather than a distinct protective mechanism. This does not undermine the pruning method, but the causal/biological analogy is stronger than what the presented evidence supports.

comments.title: Training-resource advantage over NAS/KD is asserted without measuring ASSL training cost
comments.quote: With our aligned structured sparsity learning strategy, we do not have to search lots of architectures or train a teacher network, which usually consume plenty of extra computation resources.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended contrast: ASSL is a pruning/regularization procedure, so it avoids two well-known sources of overhead in competing compression approaches, namely NAS search and teacher-student distillation. The earlier introduction also gives a concrete example of NAS being expensive, so the general intuition is reasonable. What remains insufficiently supported in this passage is the implied training-resource advantage of ASSL itself. The table reports inference-side quantities—parameters, Mult-Adds, and PSNR—but not the cost of obtaining ASSLN. From the method description, ASSL adds a sparsity-learning stage, sparsity-structure alignment optimization, pruning, and finetuning, and elsewhere a large number of iterations is mentioned for alignment/sparsity learning. Without reporting GPU-days, training iterations, or memory use for ASSL versus the NAS/KD baselines, the claim that this route avoids “plenty of extra computation resources” is plausible but not demonstrated quantitatively. A careful reader could accept that ASSL avoids architecture search and teacher training, but not necessarily that its total training cost is lower by the claimed practical margin.

comments.title: Conclusion overstates comparison as against all recent state-of-the-art methods
comments.quote: Our ASSLN achieves superior performance over recent state-of-the-art methods quantitatively and qualitatively.
comments.explanation: The experimental framing in the surrounding text is mainly about lightweight SR networks and model-compression baselines, and within that scope the reported tables appear to support a favorable performance/resource trade-off for ASSLN. However, the conclusion drops the qualifier “lightweight” or “efficient” and says “recent state-of-the-art methods” broadly. In image super-resolution, that phrase would normally include much larger high-accuracy models such as EDSR/RCAN-style networks, which the paper itself acknowledges can have stronger absolute SR capacity at much higher cost. Thus the sentence is potentially misleading if read as an absolute PSNR/visual superiority claim over the full SR state of the art, rather than over the selected lightweight/compression baselines. This is fixable by qualifying the claim as superior among recent lightweight/efficient SR methods or in the performance-complexity trade-off.
